courses.subject: DBAC
courses.title: Introduction to Applied Business Research

schedule.class_attributes: PTH, change to Eng if needed

courses.description: The course will provide an overview on applied business research. It aims to help students to understand the process of the applied business research leading to a successful DBA thesis. The course will cover topics such as research ethics, research planning and management, research problem identification, research approach, research design, methods of data collection and data analysis. The course will also give an introduction to qualitative research methods.
courses.learning_outcomes: Students will understand research ethics, as well as the process of applied business research for doctoral studies, including research planning, problem identification, methodologies and design, data collection and analysis.
courses.course_syllabus: To be assigned by course teacher
courses.feedback_evaluation: Mandatory Course and Teaching Evaluation (CTE) is conducted at the end of the course.
courses.required_readings: To be assigned by course teacher
courses.recommended_readings: To be assigned by course teacher

courses.subject: DBAC
courses.title: Quantitative Methods for Applied Business Studies

schedule.class_attributes: PTH, change to Eng if needed

courses.description:
By acquainting students with core quantitative methods, this course aims to help students utilize basic statistical tools and techniques appropriately for applied business research leading to a successful DBA thesis. The course will be practical rather than theoretical. Students will learn useful quantitative skills through numerous examples in business management. The main topics cover:
1. Research question specification and hypothesis development suitable for doctoral level applied business research
2. Data collection and organization
3. Approaches to describe and summarize data statistically in terms of central tendency, dispersion, correlation, etc.
4. Hypothesis testing and inferences using econometric methods including bivariate and multiple regressions
5. Result interpretation and presentation using tables and figures.
courses.learning_outcomes: This course aims to introduce relevant statistical and other analytical tools and concepts that are applicable to applied business research for doctoral studies. Emphases are on understanding and application of the quantitative skills to test the research questions.
courses.course_syllabus: To be assigned by course teacher
courses.feedback_evaluation: Mandatory Course and Teaching Evaluation (CTE) is conducted at the end of the course.
courses.required_readings: To be assigned by course teacher
courses.recommended_readings: To be assigned by course teacher

courses.subject: DBAC
courses.title: Surveys and Experimental Methods for Applied Business Studies

meetings.instructor:
Professor DAI Xianchi, 
Professor KWONG Yuk Yee Jessica

schedule.class_attributes: PTH, change to Eng if needed

courses.description:
This course aims at helping students to understand how to generate and test theories in applied business research using surveys and experimental methods. Students will learn skills in designing surveys and experiments, collecting and analysing behavioural data. The course will discuss how these methods advance our practical and scientific knowledge in business research suitable for a DBA thesis. The main topics cover:
1. Theories about theory
2. Research question specification and hypothesis development
3. Different types of survey and experimental design
4. Different types of behavioural data
5. Hypothesis testing and statistical inferences
6. Result interpretation and presentation
courses.learning_outcomes: Students will be familiar with how to use surveys and experimental methods to generate and test theories, design surveys and experiments, identify, collect and analyse behavioral data.

courses.description: This course provides surveys on frontier applied business management research issues in various fields of business management. The course will cover relevant topics in selected economics and business disciplines such as Accounting, Finance and Managerial Economics.
courses.learning_outcomes: Students should be able to gain insights on various aspects of applied business management issues related to accounting, financing, investment, and managerial economics. Students should be able to understand relevant concepts for their doctoral thesis proposal writing.

courses.subject: DBAC
courses.title: Advanced Topics in Global Operations and Supply Chain Strategies

schedule.class_attributes: PTH, change to Eng if needed

courses.description: This course aims to bridge the traditional operations management theories with cutting-edge topics on how companies adjust their global operations and supply chains in the face of new challenges such as geo-political tensions, trade barriers, natural disasters and other megatrends such as global supply chain restructuring, technology decoupling, and business sustainability.
courses.learning_outcomes: Students would be able to understand traditional theories and research topics on operations management and apply theories to practice in the face of new challenges and analyze the effectiveness of operations management.

courses.subject: DBAC
courses.title: Advanced Topics in Marketing Strategy

schedule.class_attributes: PTH, change to Eng if needed

courses.description: This course introduces various methodologies for developing better marketing strategies through insightful understanding of consumers and markets. Examples include A/B testing and business experimentation, data-driven models and algorithms, lab experimentation, and applied game theory.
courses.learning_outcomes: Students will understand consumers and markets based on classical methodologies, and be able to apply these various methodologies to generate and operate marketing strategies.

courses.subject: DBAC
courses.title: Advanced Topics in Business Artificial Intelligence

schedule.class_attributes: PTH, change to Eng if needed
PTH, change to Eng if needed

courses.description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of the rapid development of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies and their business applications. It focuses on how advances in natural language processing, computer vision, generative AI, and reinforcement learning are reshaping business landscapes, while also examining the new challenges posed by data security and ethical standards in AI. By introducing the underlying technological development of AI, the course addresses key questions facing enterprises today: which business functions can leverage AI to reduce costs and increase efficiency; what new growth opportunities can be created through AI adoption; and, looking ahead, how human society and AI technologies can evolve in synergy.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completing this course, students will be able to understand, explain, and apply the following:

* Stages of frontier AI development: from chatbots to autonomous agents, to silicon-based organizations, and ultimately to embodied intelligence.
* First principles of AI technologies (Scaling Law + The Bitter Lesson): the interplay of large models, massive computing power, and big data.
* First principles of AI commercialization: reducing the marginal cost of distributing intelligence, while organically integrating scale with personalization.
* AI-native business models in the new era: from one-person business to one-person unicorn.
* The most important long-term trade-off of AI: efficiency vs. safety.
courses.course_syllabus: To be assigned by course teacher
courses.feedback_evaluation: Mandatory Course and Teaching Evaluation (CTE) is conducted at the end of the course.
courses.required_readings: To be assigned by course teacher
courses.recommended_readings: To be assigned by course teacher

courses.subject: DBAC
courses.title: Web3 and Crypto Finance

schedule.class_attributes: PTH, change to Eng if needed

courses.description:
This course aims to help students understand the latest trends and developments in global capital markets and the financial sector. Focusing on the rise of the blockchain technology and the third-generation Internet (Web3), it emphasizes the current development and application of digital assets and crypto finance in the Web 3 space.

The core theme of the course is to understand the blockchain technology, smart contract, and their applications in decentralized finance (DeFi) and other Web3 business innovations (DApps). blockchain technology and smart contracts are the building blocks for a more secure, transparent, and efficient digital world. Blockchain is a distributed ledger technology with very special features such as immutability and a high level of security. Smart contract are digital contracts allowing terms contingent on decentralized consensus that are tamper-proof and typically self-enforcing through automated execution.

In addition, the course covers key cryptography concepts (such as hashing, digital signatures, and asymmetric cryptography). It also covers topics on the principles and recent development of stablecoins and Real-World Asset (RWA) tokenization.
courses.learning_outcomes:

courses.title: Thesis Research

courses.description: Students will be required to take this course every term from the time of the advancement to doctoral candidature throughout their thesis preparation. The supervisor will meet the student on a regular basis and advise the student on all matters related to thesis research. Under the supervision of the supervisor(s), students are required to employ applied research methods to conceptualize the research idea, review appropriate literature and carry out research.
courses.learning_outcomes: The goal of this course is to monitor students' progress in thesis and research. Upon completing this course, students will develop a skill to summarise the essential ingredients of a paper in a concise way, to develop a research topic and conduct applied research using what they have learnt in other courses.
courses.course_syllabus: To be assigned by course teacher
courses.feedback_evaluation: Feedback for evaluation can be done through the individual meetings between the supervisor and the student.
